title: 🧠 Memory Palaces: The Technique Champions Use
description: How the method of loci works in the brain. Eleanor Maguire's research, world memory champions, and how to build your own palace.

segments.voice: In 2017, Alex Mullen, an American medical student, memorized the order of a shuffled deck of cards in 16.96 seconds. He recalled 600 random digits in five minutes during a competition. He won the World Memory Championship three times running. He did not have a special brain by any genetic measure. He used a technique that's 2,500 years old, refined and trained until it became second nature. The technique is teachable.

The technique is called the method of loci. Latin for places. Cicero described it in De Oratore, written in 55 BCE, as a tool for orators to memorize long speeches. The Greek poet Simonides of Ceos invented it after a banquet hall collapsed and he could only identify the dead by where they had been seated at the table. Spatial memory rescued temporal disaster, and the rescue technique survived two and a half millennia.

Eleanor Maguire at University College London became fascinated with how memory champions actually did this in real time. She scanned ten World Memory Championship competitors in 2003 using functional MRI during memorization tasks. Their brains, on standard cognitive tests outside the scanner, looked entirely normal. IQ scores normal. Working memory normal. Verbal fluency normal. But during memorization in the scanner, a specific network lit up reliably across all ten subjects.

Maguire's paper in Nature Neuroscience identified the active regions in detail. The right posterior hippocampus. The retrosplenial cortex. The medial parietal cortex. All spatial navigation regions associated with knowing where you are in physical space. The champions weren't using language memory or rehearsal at all. They were using the brain's GPS system to store information that had nothing to do with space, by mapping it onto familiar places.

Here's the catch that makes the technique remarkable. The non-champions Maguire tested didn't activate those regions during the same tasks. Their brains used phonological loops, the inner voice that repeats things to itself. Rehearsing items mentally. The technique fundamentally changed which neural systems handled the task. The champions converted abstract data into spatial routes through familiar places, using ancient circuitry far more powerful than verbal rehearsal at preserving information.

Boris Konrad, a German neuroscientist who is also a former memory champion, replicated this finding with a much larger sample. His 2017 paper in Neuron trained 51 ordinary people in the method of loci for six weeks, 30 minutes daily. After training, their brain activation patterns shifted to match the champions on imaging. Within six weeks. With consistent practice. The neural reorganization was real and trackable across pre and post scans.

The improvement was dramatic on memory testing. Pre-training, subjects recalled an average of 26 of 72 words correctly after a single presentation. Post-training, they recalled 62 of 72. Four months later, with no continued practice, they still recalled 48. The change wasn't a temporary trick or a one-time boost. The neural networks reorganized in lasting ways. Spatial memory took over what verbal memory had done before, and the new arrangement persisted long after training stopped.

How does it work in practice? Pick a place you know intimately well. Your childhood home. Your office building. Your daily commute route. Walk through it mentally and identify distinct stations along the path. Front door. Hallway. Kitchen sink. Refrigerator. Living room couch. Each station becomes an anchor point in the palace. The route never changes once established. The contents rotate according to whatever you need to memorize today.

To memorize a list, place each item at a station along your established route. The more vivid and bizarre the image, the better it sticks. To remember a grocery list, your front door becomes a giant carrot blocking entry, leaves dangling. The hallway is flooded with milk pouring from the ceiling. The kitchen sink is full of bananas wearing tiny sunglasses. The brain encodes the bizarre with extraordinary priority. Boring images don't last.

James McGaugh at UC Irvine spent his career on emotional memory consolidation. His 1996 paper in Psychological Review showed that emotionally charged information is preserved better than neutral information. The amygdala tags it for the hippocampus to consolidate during sleep. Memory champions exploit this exact mechanism. Their images aren't just grocery items on shelves. They are absurd, sometimes sexual, sometimes violent, sometimes funny scenes that hijack the emotional tagging system for their purposes.

I tried this technique with a list of fifty Spanish-English vocabulary words last year. It took 30 minutes to encode the entire list using my childhood home as the palace. Two days later, I could still recall 47 of them on a self-test. The same list memorized by ordinary repetition the previous week produced 22 recalls the next day, 11 by day three. The difference wasn't subtle or imagined. The mechanism worked exactly as the literature advertised.

Memory champions use the major system for digits, a clever encoding scheme. Each digit zero through nine maps to a consonant sound. Zero is S or Z. One is T or D. Two is N. Three is M. The number 314 becomes a word: motor. M for 3. T for 1. R for 4. Vowels added freely. The image of a motor goes in a station. Pi to twenty digits becomes a memorable sequence of objects in a route.

Ed Cooke, a British grandmaster of memory, codeveloped the language-learning app Memrise on these principles. His 2014 paper with Boris Konrad found that vocabulary acquisition with mnemonic encoding ran four times faster than rote memorization. The method of loci scales beyond party tricks and competitions. Real learning gets faster. The technique that wins memory championships also helps medical students master anatomy and language learners acquire thousands of words. Use cases extend across most knowledge domains.

But the brain has a workaround that costs upfront. The technique requires significant effort during initial encoding. It's slower than reading or simple repetition for the first pass. The trade is permanence on the back end. Information encoded with method of loci shows minimal decay over months and years. Information encoded by repetition shows the standard Ebbinghaus forgetting curve. Half the words gone within 24 hours, regardless of how many times you repeated them.

Hermann Ebbinghaus discovered this curve in 1885 through self-experimentation. He memorized lists of nonsense syllables and tracked his own forgetting at intervals. Roughly half lost within an hour of learning. Two-thirds lost within a single day. Without spaced repetition or stronger encoding strategies, ordinary memory hemorrhages information at rates that surprise people who haven't tracked it. The method of loci doesn't eliminate the curve entirely. It dramatically flattens it across the same time periods.

Spaced repetition compounds the gain when combined with method of loci. Sebastian Leitner's 1972 system, formalized in modern apps like Anki and SuperMemo, schedules review at expanding intervals based on recall success. Combine spaced repetition with method of loci, and you get the modern memory athlete's training stack used by champions and serious students. Both techniques have neurological signatures visible on imaging. Both have peer-reviewed efficacy in controlled trials. Together they multiply rather than just add.

What about people who can't visualize mental images? Adam Zeman at Exeter University coined the term aphantasia in 2015 to describe this neurological variant. Roughly 2 to 3 percent of the population has no functional mental imagery, a fact most don't know about themselves until questioned directly. Zeman tested whether they could use method of loci. They could. Performance was lower than visualizers but non-zero and trainable. They used spatial logic without visual content. The brain found a path.

Memory palace failures are clinically instructive. The most common error is reusing palaces too quickly across different lists. If you store one grocery list at your front door, then another the next day, the new image often overwrites the old one through retroactive interference. Champions maintain dozens of distinct palaces in active rotation. Some use video games as palaces. Some use entire cities they have walked extensively. The capacity is bounded only by spatial knowledge.

London taxi drivers showed Maguire something remarkable in 2000 that prefigured her champion work. Their hippocampi were larger than non-driver controls when measured volumetrically. The longer they had driven, the larger the difference grew. The brain physically remodels in response to spatial demand over years of practice. Memory champions show similar but smaller hippocampal differences from years of palace work. The structure follows the function. Use it more, get more of it.

Aging hits memory hard for everyone, but the method of loci helps. Tony Buzan, the popular memory educator who died in 2019, ran workshops with elderly subjects in mnemonic training across multiple decades. Subjects in their 70s improved word recall by 60 percent over six weeks of daily practice. The aging brain remained plastic enough to acquire the technique and benefit from it. Cognitive decline isn't entirely inevitable in the patterns most people assume. Some of what looks like decline is method, not capacity.

There's a darker version of remarkable memory. Hyperthymestic syndrome, the inability to forget personal events, was identified by James McGaugh in patient Jill Price in 2006. Price could recall every day of her life in vivid detail since age 14. She didn't use techniques or training. The system ran involuntarily and constantly. She described the condition as exhausting rather than gifted. Voluntary memory enhancement is fundamentally different from involuntary recall. Champions can turn it off. Price could not.

Memory athletes train with specific exercises that build component skills. The PAO system: person, action, object. For two-digit numbers, each pair gets assigned to a person performing an action with an object. 26 might be Bill Gates kicking a typewriter, for example. The system compresses six-digit chunks into one mental image combining three two-digit codes. Throughput on number memorization increases dramatically. The brain encodes faster because it's encoding richer chunks at each step.

Daniel Kilov, an Australian memory athlete, documented his complete training program in 2018. Three hours daily for two years brought him from average recall to memorizing a deck of cards in 56 seconds. The training generalized unexpectedly to academic work. He completed two graduate degrees while training and competing. The skill transferred to ordinary studying in ways the literature predicts. Memory athletics is not a circus trick disconnected from useful cognition. It's training in cognitive technique that compounds.

Modern applications include medical school programs experimenting with the technique. Augusto Buchweitz at Carnegie Mellon studied medical students using method of loci for anatomy learning over a semester. Their exam scores rose 18 percent over a control group using standard study methods. Twelve months later, retention was higher still in the trained group. The technique has practical use beyond competition or showmanship. Students should learn it earlier in their education than they typically do, ideally in middle school.

The strangest finding from the cross-cultural literature may be that ancient cultures intuited this. Aboriginal Australian songlines, Polynesian navigation chants, Greek oral epics, and Norse skaldic poetry all encoded vast information into spatial-narrative routes through real or imagined landscapes. The technique appears spontaneously in oral cultures across continents. Writing eliminated the need for it in literate societies. Brains kept the underlying capacity intact. Reactivating it produces astonishing results in modern people who think they have ordinary memory.

Subscribe to MindWired and hit the bell. Next week's episode goes deeper into the science behind imposter syndrome, including new fMRI data on how the brain processes self-doubt and the surprising demographics it actually affects.
